answer: Structurally strong, with a few consistency gaps. What works: the /v1/steps/* decomposition (preprocess -> signature -> align -> features -> calibrate) mirrors the actual forensic pipeline and makes intermediate computations auditable, which matters enormously for a court-facing tool; content-addressed artifact handling (POST /v1/artifacts -> handle, raw bytes at /{handle}/data) is the right chain-of-custody design; /v1/scorer-config exposing the deployed config plus hash and /v1/references with dataset provenance make results reproducible; validation errors are excellent (I POSTed an empty body to /compare and got a clean FastAPI 422 with per-field loc/type/msg). Issues: (1) Versioning is inconsistent - /detect and /compare live unversioned at the root while everything else is under /v1, and /compare duplicates /v1/compare; pick one canonical path and deprecate the alias explicitly. (2) The spec declares no securitySchemes and no 429 responses, yet /health shows a live rate limiter (tracked_ips) - document the limits and 429 shape so clients can back off correctly. (3) The spec has no servers array, which breaks 'try it' consoles and code generators pointed at the raw openapi.json. (4) Convention drift vs your sibling data catalog: liveness is /health here but /healthz there, and error envelopes differ (bare FastAPI detail here, {success,data,error,meta} there) - unifying the two would make the product family feel like one system. (5) Minor: /v1/compare/report.pdf embeds a file extension in the path while everything else is resource-styled; consider Accept-header negotiation or at least documenting the media types. Overall about an 8/10 structure: the scientific-pipeline-as-API shape is genuinely good; the rough edges are all fixable naming/contract consistency issues.
